A 2022 Cell Reports Medicine study by Enrico Coiera and Sidong Liu finds that pre-pandemic, this lag led to significant unnecessary waste across the healthcare system of up to $274 billion per year globally. Tiffany Brooks and colleagues establish, in the Journal of Pain Research in 2020, that cPP affects approximately 15% of women worldwide and has significant psychological, physical and financial impact on the lives of sufferers, and in NAM Perspectives in 2022, Sergio Aguilar-Gaxiola and colleagues show that a conceptual model should consider that health is not just about being free of disease or infirmity, but that individuals and communities have the right to thrive—to reach the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of health. The prevalence of chronic pelvic pain and the emphasis on thriving rather than merely disease-free status suggest that caring professions involve relational and psychosocial dimensions that task-level AI impact metrics do not capture, limiting the applicability of patent-similarity scores to occupations whose core value lies in therapeutic presence.

The evidence above is drawn at the task level; the evidence below is drawn at the economy level. The evidence Aman Kumar and colleagues present in the JOURNAL GLOBAL VALUES in 2023 is that AI in the realm of education offers sophisticated instructional methodologies to students through the utilization of data mining techniques, intelligent teaching platforms, and personalized learning systems. Writing in the Interdisciplinary Journal of Innovation in Nepalese Academia in 2025, Sagar Bishwakarma and Smeeth Bista show that a significant majority (77.17%) are studying in public higher education institutions, while Silah Hayati Kamsani and Darren Maing Anak Abitt, writing in the International Journal of Research and Innovation in Social Science in 2025, report that the QR code-based system demonstrated low implementation costs (RM 768.25/USD 62 per laboratory station) using standard office equipment and basic camera modules, making automation education accessible within typical Malaysian institutional budgets. Felix Aberu and Tersia Botha, writing in Acta Academiae Beregsasiensis Economics in 2026, report that unemployment among individuals with advanced education exhibits a strong negative effect on sustainable development in the short run, with a coefficient of −1.025 (p = 0.027), implying that a 1% increase in unemployment among highly educated workers reduces sustainable development by approximately 1.02%.

The evidence to this point is measured at the economy level; what follows is measured at the worker level. A 2024 Artificial Intelligence Review study by Francisco Bolaños and colleagues finds that only eight of the evaluated tools implement at least 70% of the designated features, with DistillerSR, Nested Knowledge, Dextr, and ExaCT leading with the highest feature coverage at 82%, while Matthew J Page and colleagues find, in their 2021 BMJ paper, that this approach can increase review efficiency by enabling the review team to start on subsequent steps of the review while less relevant records are still being screened. Mauro Cazzaniga and colleagues establish, in Economía in 2025, that the negative impact is more pronounced for Brazilian workers compared to those in the UK, with college-educated individuals facing a –27.9 percent vs. –24.1 percent impact, and non-college-educated workers seeing a –31.7 percent vs. –28.5 percent impact, respectively. The evidence Yolanda González-Castro and colleagues present in the Journal of Infrastructure Policy and Development in 2025 is that a total of 367 unique documents were identified in both WoS and Scopus, underscoring the depth and breadth of research in this area. The volume of identified research on new-age professions bounds the scope of existing evidence; while growing, it remains insufficient to resolve questions about long-run occupational transformation in healthcare and education, where task-level displacement metrics and tool-feature evaluations capture only fragments of a larger restructuring process.

### Creative work and the generative-AI frontier

Writing in the Columbia Journal of Tax Law in 2025, Jeremy Bearer-Friend and Sarah Polcz show that law, medicine, and creative industries are set to be among the first fields impacted, while Leonardo Banh and Gero Strobel find, in their 2023 Electronic Markets paper, that generative AI finds its utility across various modalities, including the generation of text, image, video, code, sound, and other produced content, such as molecules or 3D renderings. Thomas Montefiore and colleagues, writing in the Journal of Business Ethics in 2026, report that according to 2011 Australian census data, creative industries with very high creative intensity, such as motion picture and video production (76% creative intensity), will likely have more creative work impacted by AI than creative industries with lower creative intensity, such as museum operations (27% creative intensity), while Jainendra Kumar Verma and Kamal Krishna, writing in the International Journal of Foreign Trade and International Business in 2026, report that generative AI redesigns work even without formal redesign: it shifts the locus of human contribution from production to specifying, evaluating, and correcting machine output, raising the premium on judgment just as it lowers the premium on routine cognitive execution.

The overreliance evidence bounds the judgment-premium thesis advanced in the work-redesign literature: if sustained delegation to generative systems erodes the very decision-making capacities that the redesign reallocates toward human judgment, the productivity gains documented in early experiments may not translate into durable capability expansion for workers in creative fields. The sectoral-intensity evidence remains limited by its inattention to within-culture and across-culture factors that prescribe how work and home roles are shared between the sexes: the gendered and cultural dimensions of who enters creative occupations, and who bears the consequences of AI disruption within them, are unresolved by the current evidence.

## Critical Synthesis: What the Literature Establishes and What It Does Not

### What the evidence establishes

Regional displacement metrics similarly diverge from firm- and worker-level outcomes, establishing that spatial aggregates and individual labor experiences capture distinct dimensions of employment restructuring. A 2023 npj Urban Sustainability study by Roberta Capello and Camilla Lenzi finds that the regional marginal effect of robot density on employment share for the top 25% of cities is 0.003, while in İnsan ve Toplum Bilimleri Araştırmaları Dergisi in 2025, Mustafa Ergün show that worker-level informal employment declined only moderately from 27.6% in 2019 to 19.2% in 2023. These income findings are not contradictory in studies of occupations: the two estimates observe different units of analysis, and the evidence establishes the effect at the occupation level without establishing it at the economy level.

### What the evidence cannot yet resolve

The literature on AI's restructuring of employment cannot yet resolve whether the magnitude of displacement observed at one unit of analysis generalizes to another, because region-, firm-, industry-, and worker-level studies employ fundamentally different identification strategies and outcome measures that are not commensurable. Roberta Capello and Camilla Lenzi establish, in npj Urban Sustainability in 2023, that robot density is associated with a −0.005*** decline in the employment-to-population ratio for top 25% cities, while robot density growth is associated with a −0.109** decline for the same group, while Nicolae Istudor and colleagues find, in their 2025 Journal of Business Economics and Management paper, that the perception that acquired personal AI skills influence overall AI impact is moderated by the risk of not finding a job because of AI (β = –0.167, t-value = 3.665, p < 0.001). The evidence Roberta Capello and Camilla Lenzi present in npj Urban Sustainability in 2023 is that robot density growth is associated with a −0.136*** decline in employment to population ratio for non-city areas at the top 10% city grouping, while Musa Nouman Siddiqui and Dr Nasir Khan, writing in the ACADEMIA International Journal for Social Sciences in 2025, report that AI-driven news creation technologies have led to some organizations reporting up to a 50% reduction in employment expenditures.

## Implications and a Research Agenda

### Implications that follow from the mechanisms

Writing in the London journal of interdisciplinary sciences in 2023, Adam Sharif and colleagues show that jobs susceptible to full AI takeover make up 6.3% of the labor force, and if AI fully takes over, millions of jobs will be lost, bringing job instability. Hasraddin Guliyev, writing in Research in Globalization in 2023, report that the unemployment rate reduces by 0.003 percent as the search volume related to artificial intelligence increases in 24 high-tech developed countries.

The evidence above is drawn at the occupation level; the evidence below is drawn at the industry level. A 2025 Applied Computer Science study by Oxana DENISSOVA and colleagues finds that the transformed production volume variable has the strongest influence, accounting for approximately 58.3% of the total predictive power, highlighting the scale effect associated with production expansion and its direct impact on employment dynamics. Atullya Tripathi establish, in the Alliance Journal of Corporate and Commercial Law in 2025, that AI algorithms are educated on data; if that data represents current society prejudices, the AI systems will reinforce and even amplify these prejudices, and discriminatory results in recruiting, promotions, and other HR procedures may follow from this, while in Archives for Technical Sciences in 2025, E Pavithra show that AI-based recruitment, performance measurement, and decision-support systems have a tremendous positive influence on operational efficiency, employee productivity, and strategic alignment. The evidence Adeyemi Olatunbosun and colleagues present in the Journal of economics, business and commerce in 2025 is that social protection mechanisms—unemployment benefits, job criteria, and wage stabilizations—are required to support the workers who will be removed by automation and assist them in adapting to new jobs. Writing in Izzivi prihodnosti in 2025, Alexander van Biezen show that at the societal level, the research underlines the urgency of open policy debates and ethical reflection on AI regulation, and addressing the implications of AI autonomy is essential to preserve human agency, democratic accountability, and social justice in the digital era.

The research agenda that follows from these mechanisms is constrained by where evidence has been collected, how it has been measured, and whether the relational architecture of workplaces—now shown to moderate performance and well-being outcomes—has been adequately integrated into models of AI's restructuring effects. Trinh Nguyen and Amany Elbanna, writing in Information Systems Frontiers in 2025, report that further longitudinal and case study research is needed to explore the nuances of how AI automation and AI augmentation occur in practice and the implications this may have for workers and organisations. The calls for longitudinal designs, primary workplace data, and methodologically rigorous observation jointly establish that the field cannot yet adjudicate whether AI augmentation mitigates or exacerbates within-occupation inequality, because existing evidence has neither tracked the same workers through actual transitions nor measured workplace conditions at sufficient resolution to distinguish precarious from higher-quality employment outcomes.

### Gaps the evidence itself exposes

The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of job loss across different units of analysis (worker versus industry) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of job loss across different units of analysis (firm versus industry) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (industry versus firm) and is not directly comparable. The productivity finding in studies of employment rests on a observational design in one case and a quasi-experimental design in the other; the evidence therefore establishes it more securely than either study alone. The efficiency finding in studies of improve rests on a survey design in one case and a quasi-experimental design in the other; the evidence therefore establishes it more securely than either study alone. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of efficiency across different units of analysis (firm versus worker) and is not directly comparable.

content.content:
1	# The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labour Market: A Literature Review
2	
3	## TL;DR
4	
5	- **The academic literature is genuinely divided but reconcilable:** AI and automation reliably raise productivity and, on cognitive tasks, disproportionately help less-skilled workers, yet the same task-based mechanics show automation displacing labour, depressing the labour share, and — where innovation is biased away from augmentation — driving a large share of wage inequality.
6	- **Generative AI represents a structural break from prior automation waves:** whereas robots and software targeted routine and manual work, LLM exposure is concentrated in high-wage, white-collar, cognitive, and creative occupations — but measured *exposure* has not yet translated into detectable aggregate wage or employment effects.
7	- **Outcomes are a policy and design choice, not technological destiny:** the decisive variable is whether AI is steered toward augmenting labour and creating new tasks or toward pure displacement, which makes the direction of innovation, reskilling institutions, and tax/innovation policy the central levers.
8	
9	## Key Findings
10	
11	1. **The task-based framework unifies the debate.** Nearly all rigorous work builds on the model in which jobs are bundles of tasks and technology competes with or complements labour at the task level. Net labour-market outcomes depend on the balance between a *displacement effect* and countervailing *productivity* and *reinstatement* effects.
12	
13	2. **Robots raised productivity but pressured vulnerable workers.** Graetz & Michaels found productivity gains and no aggregate hours loss across 17 countries; Acemoglu & Restrepo found sharp local displacement in US labour markets.
14	
15	3. **Generative AI compresses the skill distribution.** In three convergent studies, AI raised average productivity while helping novices and lower-ability workers most — a "skill-levelling" pattern that is the strongest empirical case for optimism.
16	
17	4. **AI's distributional direction is contested.** Historical automation polarized the labour market by hollowing out middle-skill routine work; AI, being directed at high-skilled tasks, could in principle *reduce* 90:10 inequality — an open, unresolved question.
18	
19	5. **Exposure ≠ realised impact.** Exposure indices measure technical potential, not equilibrium outcomes; as of the latest evidence, aggregate effects on AI-exposed occupations were too small to detect.
20	
21	## Details
22	
23	### 1. Positive Views: Productivity Enhancement and Skill Democratization
24	
25	A substantial strand argues that automation and AI raise productivity, complement labour, and sustain or expand labour demand. **Autor**, in a widely-cited *Journal of Economic Perspectives* essay, argues that commentators systematically overstate machine substitution and understate complementarity: automation raises productivity, lowers prices, and increases demand for the non-automated tasks in which humans retain comparative advantage — problem-solving, adaptability, and creativity. This complementarity explains "why there are still so many jobs" despite two centuries of mechanisation.
26	
27	The task framework of **Acemoglu & Restrepo** formalises the positive channel through the *reinstatement effect*: the creation of new labour-intensive tasks shifts the task content of production back toward labour, raising both the labour share and labour demand. In their full model, automation and new-task creation can proceed hand-in-hand along a stable balanced-growth path.
28	
29	Empirical robot research offers support. **Graetz & Michaels**, using panel data on robot adoption in seventeen countries, estimate that increased robot use raised annual labour-productivity growth and total factor productivity, lowered output prices, and did not significantly reduce total hours worked — although they find reductions in the low-skilled employment share. **Bessen**, examining two centuries of US textile, steel, and automotive data, shows automation historically *increased* industry employment when product demand was elastic, because productivity-driven price declines expanded output faster than labour was saved.
30	
31	The most striking recent evidence concerns the *skill-democratization* effect of generative AI:
32	- **Brynjolfsson, Li & Raymond**, studying the staggered rollout of a generative-AI conversational assistant among more than 5,000 customer-support agents, find that "access to the tool increases productivity, as measured by issues resolved per hour, by 14% on average, including a 34% improvement for novice and low-skilled workers but with minimal impact on experienced and highly skilled workers." They interpret this as the AI disseminating the tacit knowledge of the most able workers.
33	- **Noy & Zhang**, in a preregistered online experiment with 444 college-educated professionals performing mid-level writing tasks, find that "ChatGPT substantially raises average productivity: time taken decreases by 0.8 SDs and output quality rises by 0.4 SDs. Inequality between workers decreases, as ChatGPT compresses the productivity distribution by benefiting low-ability workers more."
34	- **Peng et al.** report that software developers randomly given GitHub Copilot "completed the task 55.8% faster (95% confidence interval: 21–89%)" — a mean of 71.17 minutes for the treatment group versus 160.89 minutes for the control (p = 0.0017) — with developers who had less programming experience, older programmers, and those who program more hours per day benefiting the most.
35	
36	**Methodological note:** these positive findings rest on different foundations — conceptual/theoretical; observational IV panel studies; and randomised experiments. The experiments offer clean causal identification but on narrow, short-duration tasks, limiting generalisation to real workplaces over time.
37	
38	### 2. Negative Views: Labour Displacement and Wage-Deflation Risks
39	
40	The pessimistic literature emphasises displacement, wage stagnation, declining labour share, and rising inequality. The seminal alarm came from **Frey & Osborne**, who used a Gaussian-process classifier over 702 occupations and concluded that "about 47 percent of total US employment is at risk … jobs we expect could be automated relatively soon, perhaps over the next decade or two." Their occupation-level (rather than task-level) approach has been criticised for overstating automatability, and subsequent task-based estimates produced smaller shares — but the paper crystallised the debate.
41	
42	Theoretically, **Acemoglu & Restrepo** show that automation *always* reduces the labour share in value added and can reduce labour demand and wages even while raising productivity, via the displacement effect; whether this is offset depends on the strength of reinstatement and productivity effects. Their decomposition attributes sluggish recent employment growth to accelerating displacement, weakening reinstatement, and slower productivity growth.
43	
44	The most influential displacement evidence is **Acemoglu & Restrepo**: studying US commuting zones, they find that "one more robot per thousand workers reduces the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42%" at the national level, with even larger effects (about 0.39 percentage points and 0.77%) in the most exposed local labour markets, and no offsetting positive effects detectable in exposed zones. **Autor & Salomons**, using four decades of harmonised cross-country industry data, find automation is labour-share-displacing in originating industries, and that this effect intensified over time as the wage response to productivity growth weakened.
45	
46	Acemoglu & Restrepo introduce the concept of *"so-so automation"* — technologies just good enough to displace workers but not productive enough to generate large productivity or reinstatement gains (e.g., automated customer service, self-checkout). In **"The Wrong Kind of AI?"**, they argue the commercial direction of AI has been excessively biased toward automation rather than augmentation, with consequences of stagnating labour demand, declining labour share, rising inequality, and disappointing productivity growth.
47	
48	The inequality consequences are quantified in **Acemoglu & Restrepo**, who attribute "between 50% and 70% of changes in the U.S. wage structure over the last four decades … to relative wage declines of worker groups specialized in routine tasks in industries experiencing rapid automation." This links automation directly to the polarization documented by Autor.
49	
50	A crucial nuance distinguishes AI from earlier automation: **Webb** — using textual overlap between patents and job-task descriptions — finds AI is directed at *high-skilled* tasks and projects that AI could *reduce* 90:10 wage inequality (by pressuring high earners) while leaving the top 1% unaffected. Webb's paper circulated as an SSRN working paper and was subsequently withdrawn; it is cited here as a working paper.
51	
52	**Methodological note:** this literature is dominated by quasi-experimental designs exploiting exposure variation and structural task models. A recurring limitation is external validity: robots are tangible, rivalrous capital, whereas AI is intangible and non-rivalrous, so robot-era estimates may not transfer to AI.
53	
54	### 3. Specific Challenges of the Generative-AI Transition
55	
56	The emergence of large language models (LLMs) prompted a distinct question: unlike prior waves affecting routine and manual work, generative AI appears to expose white-collar, cognitive, and creative occupations.
57	
58	**Brynjolfsson, Mitchell & Rock** anticipated this. Constructing a "Suitability for Machine Learning" (SML) measure over 18,156 O*NET tasks, they found that (i) ML affects *different* occupations than earlier automation; (ii) most occupations contain at least some SML tasks; (iii) few occupations are fully automatable by ML; and (iv) realising ML's potential usually requires redesigning job task content. **Felten, Raj & Seamans** developed the AI Occupational Exposure (AIOE) index by linking AI applications to human abilities, finding highest exposure in information-processing service industries and lowest in manual, blue-collar work; their generative-AI update concludes that highly-educated, highly-paid, white-collar occupations are most exposed.
59	
60	The landmark exposure study is **Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin & Rock, "GPTs are GPTs"**. Using a rubric applied by both human experts and GPT-4, they estimate that "around 80% of the U.S. workforce could have at least 10% of their work tasks affected by the introduction of LLMs, while approximately 19% of workers may see at least 50% of their tasks impacted." Exposure *rises* with wage and education — the reverse of prior automation. They further find that "with access to an LLM, about 15% of all worker tasks in the US could be completed significantly faster at the same level of quality [and w]hen incorporating software and tooling built on top of LLMs, this share increases to between 47 and 56% of all tasks," concluding that LLMs exhibit the hallmarks of a general-purpose technology while explicitly declining to predict adoption timelines.
61	
62	The break from earlier waves is grounded in **Agrawal, Gans & Goldfarb**, who reframe AI as a fall in the cost of *prediction*. Because prediction is an input to decision-making, the labour impact is ambiguous: automating prediction can substitute for workers (where prediction is the bottleneck) or complement them by raising the value of human *judgment* and surrounding tasks. The radiologist — AI predicting from images while the physician exercises judgment — is their canonical example.
63	
64	Early experimental and observational evidence consistently shows large productivity gains on cognitive tasks with a skill-compressing pattern. The authors themselves stress limitations: narrow tasks, short horizons, no equilibrium adjustment, difficult quality measurement, and no direct mapping from task productivity to wages, employment, or the augment-versus-displace choice.
65	
66	**Measurement challenges** pervade the field. Exposure indices measure *technical potential* or *task overlap*, not realised outcomes, and are explicitly not designed to predict equilibrium wages, employment, or whether AI augments or automates. **Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell & Restrepo**, studying the near-universe of US online vacancies from 2010, find that AI-exposed establishments adopting AI reduce non-AI hiring and change skill requirements, but that aggregate effects on employment and wages were, over their sample, too small to detect. This gap between exposure and outcomes is the central empirical uncertainty of the generative-AI transition.
67	
68	### 4. Future Opportunities and Labour-Market Transformation
69	
70	The strongest evidence on new-task creation is **Autor, Chin, Salomons & Seegmiller, "New Frontiers"**, who build an eight-decade database of new job titles linked to Census microdata and patent-based innovation measures. They find that "the majority of current employment is in new job specialties introduced after 1940," but that the locus of new-work creation has shifted — from middle-paid production and clerical occupations to high-paid professional and, secondarily, low-paid service occupations. Crucially, they distinguish *augmentation* innovations (which raise occupational labour demand) from *automation* innovations (which depress it), finding that "although flows of augmentation and automation innovations are positively correlated across occupations, the former boosts occupational labor demand while the latter depresses it," and that the demand-eroding effects of automation intensified over the last four decades while the demand-increasing effects of augmentation did not. New work is real and continual, but its recent composition and the intensifying force of automation are less favourable to labour than in the mid-twentieth century.
71	
72	The **human–AI complementarity thesis** suggests the future turns on redesigning jobs so AI handles prediction/routine components while humans concentrate on judgment, interpersonal, and creative tasks. Brynjolfsson, Mitchell & Rock emphasise that capturing ML's value requires deliberate task-bundle redesign rather than wholesale occupational replacement. In medical imaging, **Ahn et al.**, in a cohort study of 497 chest radiographs read by six readers, found AI-aided interpretation reduced reporting time by about 10% (from 40.8 to 36.9 seconds per case) while improving reader sensitivity — a concrete demonstration of complementarity in a high-skilled occupation.
73	
74	On **policy**, the most developed academic argument is Acemoglu & Restrepo's contention that the *direction* of AI development is a choice. Because markets under-reward employment creation (owing to innovation externalities and tax policies favouring capital over labour), there is no guarantee of an efficient balance between automating and augmenting AI; policy should redirect innovation toward the "right kind of AI" that creates new labour-intensive tasks. The skill-democratization findings suggest a complementary opportunity: if AI raises less-experienced workers' productivity most, it could compress inequality and lower entry barriers to cognitive occupations — provided institutions support reskilling and diffusion.
75	
76	There is broad agreement that transitions will be disruptive even if aggregate employment is preserved: Bessen and Autor & Salomons stress that workers must move across industries, occupations, and geographies, and that these transitions can generate unemployment and wage losses for displaced groups. The policy consensus centres on education and training, portable social insurance, and redirecting AI toward augmentation.
77	
78	### 5. Summary Table of Industry / Occupational Cases
79	
80	| Study | Region | Occupation / Area | Application & Impact | Key Risks / Limitations |
81	|---|---|---|---|---|
82	| Graetz & Michaels | 17 countries (EU/US/Asia) | Manufacturing (industrial robots) | Raised labour-productivity and TFP growth, lowered output prices; no significant fall in total hours | Reduced low-skilled employment share; observational; pre-AI robot era |
83	| Acemoglu & Restrepo | US local labour markets | Manufacturing / commuting-zone employment | One robot per 1,000 workers cut employment-to-population ratio 0.2 pp and wages 0.42% nationally (0.39 pp / 0.77% locally) | No offsetting local gains; robots ≠ AI (tangible vs intangible capital) |
84	| Autor & Salomons | US + Europe, 4 decades | Cross-industry (automation via TFP) | Automation displaces labour and cuts labour share in originating industry; some offset via demand and input–output linkages | Labour-share displacement intensified over time; TFP proxy is coarse |
85	| Bessen | US, ~200 years | Textile, steel, automotive | Automation raised industry employment when demand elastic; job losses only after demand satiation | Historical; modern demand elasticities differ across industries |
86	| Brynjolfsson, Li & Raymond | US (Fortune 500 firm) | Customer-support agents | Generative-AI assistant raised issues-resolved-per-hour 14% on average; 34% for novices; minimal for experts | Single firm; short horizon; tacit-knowledge diffusion mechanism suggestive |
87	| Noy & Zhang | US (online, n = 444) | Mid-level professional writing | ChatGPT cut time −0.8 SD, raised quality +0.4 SD, compressed productivity distribution | Narrow tasks; lab-style experiment; substitutes for effort more than skill |
88	| Peng et al. | US/global (95 freelance developers) | Software developers | GitHub Copilot users 55.8% faster (71.2 vs 160.9 min); larger gains for less-experienced/older | Single stylised coding task; no code-quality or long-run measure |
89	| Eloundou et al. | US workforce | White-collar / cognitive occupations | ~80% of workers could have ≥10% of tasks affected by LLMs; ~19% ≥50%; exposure rises with wage/education | Exposure ≠ realised outcomes; no adoption timeline; forward-looking |
90	| Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell & Restrepo | US (online vacancies) | AI-exposed establishments | AI adopters cut non-AI hiring and changed skill demands; aggregate wage/employment effects undetectable | Establishment-level only; early period; pre-generative-AI |
91	| Ahn et al. | US (Massachusetts General Hospital) | Radiology (chest X-ray) | AI-aided reading cut reporting time ~10% (40.8→36.9 s) and improved sensitivity | Small reader panel (n = 6); single-site; commercial tool |
92	| Webb | US | High-skilled occupations (patent-task overlap) | Projects AI directed at high-skilled tasks; could reduce 90:10 wage inequality | Working paper (later withdrawn); projections, not realised outcomes |
93	| Frey & Osborne | US (702 occupations) | Cross-occupational computerisation | ~47% of US employment estimated at high automation risk over 10–20 years | Occupation-level (not task-level); likely overstates automatability |
94	
95	## Recommendations
96	
97	**For researchers and analysts using this literature:**
98	- **Stage 1 — Distinguish exposure from outcome.** Treat exposure indices as measures of technical potential only. Pair every exposure claim with realised-outcome evidence. *Threshold that would change this:* the appearance of quasi-experimental studies detecting statistically significant aggregate wage/employment effects in LLM-exposed occupations would justify upgrading exposure findings to outcome claims.
99	- **Stage 2 — Weight by identification strength.** Give greatest evidentiary weight to RCTs and IV/quasi-experimental designs, and treat occupation-level susceptibility studies and projections as scenario-setting rather than forecasts.
100	- **Stage 3 — Separate the "robot" and "AI" evidence bases.** Do not transfer robot-displacement magnitudes to generative AI without justification, given the tangible/intangible capital distinction the authors themselves flag.
101	
102	**For policymakers:**
103	- Prioritise **reskilling and transition support**, since even the optimistic studies predict disruptive cross-industry/occupation transitions rather than mass unemployment.
104	- Consider **redirecting the direction of AI innovation** toward augmentation and new-task creation, addressing the tax and externality distortions identified by Acemoglu & Restrepo that bias development toward "so-so" automation.
105	- **Leverage the skill-levelling opportunity:** because generative AI disproportionately raises the productivity of novices, targeted deployment could reduce inequality — a benchmark to monitor is whether within-occupation productivity dispersion narrows as adoption scales.
106	
107	## Caveats
108	
109	- **Strict temporal cutoff.** Several works were later revised or formally published after that date. These are cited in their working-paper/preprint versions, and the post-cutoff journal versions are deliberately **not** used. Webb was subsequently withdrawn and is flagged as a working paper.
110	- **Working papers and preprints carry more uncertainty** than peer-reviewed articles; the generative-AI evidence was largely un-refereed.
111	- **Forward-looking language.** Frey & Osborne, Webb, and Eloundou et al. present *projections and potentials*, not realised effects; their headline numbers describe what *could* happen under stated assumptions, not measured labour-market outcomes. This report presents them as such.
112	- **External validity limits.** The RCTs cover narrow tasks over short horizons; the robot studies concern manufacturing and predate modern AI; and single-firm/single-site studies may not generalise.
113	- **One near-miss excluded on date grounds.** The prominent experimental radiology paper by Agarwal, Moehring, Rajpurkar & Salz, "Combining Human Expertise with Artificial Intelligence," was released after the cutoff and is therefore **not** cited; the pre-cutoff Ahn et al. cohort study is used instead for the radiology case.
114	- **The optimistic and pessimistic findings are not necessarily contradictory:** they often describe different margins (average productivity vs distributional wage effects), different technologies (generative AI vs robots), and different time horizons (short-run experiments vs multi-decade structural change).

# The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labour Market: A Literature Review

## TL;DR

- **The academic literature is genuinely divided but reconcilable:** AI and automation reliably raise productivity and, on cognitive tasks, disproportionately help less-skilled workers, yet the same task-based mechanics show automation displacing labour, depressing the labour share, and — where innovation is biased away from augmentation — driving a large share of wage inequality.
- **Generative AI represents a structural break from prior automation waves:** whereas robots and software targeted routine and manual work, LLM exposure is concentrated in high-wage, white-collar, cognitive, and creative occupations — but measured *exposure* has not yet translated into detectable aggregate wage or employment effects.
- **Outcomes are a policy and design choice, not technological destiny:** the decisive variable is whether AI is steered toward augmenting labour and creating new tasks or toward pure displacement, which makes the direction of innovation, reskilling institutions, and tax/innovation policy the central levers.

## Key Findings

1. **The task-based framework unifies the debate.** Nearly all rigorous work builds on the model in which jobs are bundles of tasks and technology competes with or complements labour at the task level. Net labour-market outcomes depend on the balance between a *displacement effect* and countervailing *productivity* and *reinstatement* effects.

2. **Robots raised productivity but pressured vulnerable workers.** Graetz & Michaels found productivity gains and no aggregate hours loss across 17 countries; Acemoglu & Restrepo found sharp local displacement in US labour markets.

3. **Generative AI compresses the skill distribution.** In three convergent studies, AI raised average productivity while helping novices and lower-ability workers most — a "skill-levelling" pattern that is the strongest empirical case for optimism.

4. **AI's distributional direction is contested.** Historical automation polarized the labour market by hollowing out middle-skill routine work; AI, being directed at high-skilled tasks, could in principle *reduce* 90:10 inequality — an open, unresolved question.

5. **Exposure ≠ realised impact.** Exposure indices measure technical potential, not equilibrium outcomes; as of the latest evidence, aggregate effects on AI-exposed occupations were too small to detect.

## Details

### 1. Positive Views: Productivity Enhancement and Skill Democratization

A substantial strand argues that automation and AI raise productivity, complement labour, and sustain or expand labour demand. **Autor**, in a widely-cited *Journal of Economic Perspectives* essay, argues that commentators systematically overstate machine substitution and understate complementarity: automation raises productivity, lowers prices, and increases demand for the non-automated tasks in which humans retain comparative advantage — problem-solving, adaptability, and creativity. This complementarity explains "why there are still so many jobs" despite two centuries of mechanisation.

The task framework of **Acemoglu & Restrepo** formalises the positive channel through the *reinstatement effect*: the creation of new labour-intensive tasks shifts the task content of production back toward labour, raising both the labour share and labour demand. In their full model, automation and new-task creation can proceed hand-in-hand along a stable balanced-growth path.

Empirical robot research offers support. **Graetz & Michaels**, using panel data on robot adoption in seventeen countries, estimate that increased robot use raised annual labour-productivity growth and total factor productivity, lowered output prices, and did not significantly reduce total hours worked — although they find reductions in the low-skilled employment share. **Bessen**, examining two centuries of US textile, steel, and automotive data, shows automation historically *increased* industry employment when product demand was elastic, because productivity-driven price declines expanded output faster than labour was saved.

The most striking recent evidence concerns the *skill-democratization* effect of generative AI:
- **Brynjolfsson, Li & Raymond**, studying the staggered rollout of a generative-AI conversational assistant among more than 5,000 customer-support agents, find that "access to the tool increases productivity, as measured by issues resolved per hour, by 14% on average, including a 34% improvement for novice and low-skilled workers but with minimal impact on experienced and highly skilled workers." They interpret this as the AI disseminating the tacit knowledge of the most able workers.
- **Noy & Zhang**, in a preregistered online experiment with 444 college-educated professionals performing mid-level writing tasks, find that "ChatGPT substantially raises average productivity: time taken decreases by 0.8 SDs and output quality rises by 0.4 SDs. Inequality between workers decreases, as ChatGPT compresses the productivity distribution by benefiting low-ability workers more."
- **Peng et al.** report that software developers randomly given GitHub Copilot "completed the task 55.8% faster (95% confidence interval: 21–89%)" — a mean of 71.17 minutes for the treatment group versus 160.89 minutes for the control (p = 0.0017) — with developers who had less programming experience, older programmers, and those who program more hours per day benefiting the most.

**Methodological note:** these positive findings rest on different foundations — conceptual/theoretical; observational IV panel studies; and randomised experiments. The experiments offer clean causal identification but on narrow, short-duration tasks, limiting generalisation to real workplaces over time.

### 2. Negative Views: Labour Displacement and Wage-Deflation Risks

The pessimistic literature emphasises displacement, wage stagnation, declining labour share, and rising inequality. The seminal alarm came from **Frey & Osborne**, who used a Gaussian-process classifier over 702 occupations and concluded that "about 47 percent of total US employment is at risk … jobs we expect could be automated relatively soon, perhaps over the next decade or two." Their occupation-level (rather than task-level) approach has been criticised for overstating automatability, and subsequent task-based estimates produced smaller shares — but the paper crystallised the debate.

Theoretically, **Acemoglu & Restrepo** show that automation *always* reduces the labour share in value added and can reduce labour demand and wages even while raising productivity, via the displacement effect; whether this is offset depends on the strength of reinstatement and productivity effects. Their decomposition attributes sluggish recent employment growth to accelerating displacement, weakening reinstatement, and slower productivity growth.

The most influential displacement evidence is **Acemoglu & Restrepo**: studying US commuting zones, they find that "one more robot per thousand workers reduces the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42%" at the national level, with even larger effects (about 0.39 percentage points and 0.77%) in the most exposed local labour markets, and no offsetting positive effects detectable in exposed zones. **Autor & Salomons**, using four decades of harmonised cross-country industry data, find automation is labour-share-displacing in originating industries, and that this effect intensified over time as the wage response to productivity growth weakened.

Acemoglu & Restrepo introduce the concept of *"so-so automation"* — technologies just good enough to displace workers but not productive enough to generate large productivity or reinstatement gains (e.g., automated customer service, self-checkout). In **"The Wrong Kind of AI?"**, they argue the commercial direction of AI has been excessively biased toward automation rather than augmentation, with consequences of stagnating labour demand, declining labour share, rising inequality, and disappointing productivity growth.

The inequality consequences are quantified in **Acemoglu & Restrepo**, who attribute "between 50% and 70% of changes in the U.S. wage structure over the last four decades … to relative wage declines of worker groups specialized in routine tasks in industries experiencing rapid automation." This links automation directly to the polarization documented by Autor.

A crucial nuance distinguishes AI from earlier automation: **Webb** — using textual overlap between patents and job-task descriptions — finds AI is directed at *high-skilled* tasks and projects that AI could *reduce* 90:10 wage inequality (by pressuring high earners) while leaving the top 1% unaffected. Webb's paper circulated as an SSRN working paper and was subsequently withdrawn; it is cited here as a working paper.

**Methodological note:** this literature is dominated by quasi-experimental designs exploiting exposure variation and structural task models. A recurring limitation is external validity: robots are tangible, rivalrous capital, whereas AI is intangible and non-rivalrous, so robot-era estimates may not transfer to AI.

### 3. Specific Challenges of the Generative-AI Transition

The emergence of large language models (LLMs) prompted a distinct question: unlike prior waves affecting routine and manual work, generative AI appears to expose white-collar, cognitive, and creative occupations.

**Brynjolfsson, Mitchell & Rock** anticipated this. Constructing a "Suitability for Machine Learning" (SML) measure over 18,156 O*NET tasks, they found that (i) ML affects *different* occupations than earlier automation; (ii) most occupations contain at least some SML tasks; (iii) few occupations are fully automatable by ML; and (iv) realising ML's potential usually requires redesigning job task content. **Felten, Raj & Seamans** developed the AI Occupational Exposure (AIOE) index by linking AI applications to human abilities, finding highest exposure in information-processing service industries and lowest in manual, blue-collar work; their generative-AI update concludes that highly-educated, highly-paid, white-collar occupations are most exposed.

The landmark exposure study is **Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin & Rock, "GPTs are GPTs"**. Using a rubric applied by both human experts and GPT-4, they estimate that "around 80% of the U.S. workforce could have at least 10% of their work tasks affected by the introduction of LLMs, while approximately 19% of workers may see at least 50% of their tasks impacted." Exposure *rises* with wage and education — the reverse of prior automation. They further find that "with access to an LLM, about 15% of all worker tasks in the US could be completed significantly faster at the same level of quality [and w]hen incorporating software and tooling built on top of LLMs, this share increases to between 47 and 56% of all tasks," concluding that LLMs exhibit the hallmarks of a general-purpose technology while explicitly declining to predict adoption timelines.

The break from earlier waves is grounded in **Agrawal, Gans & Goldfarb**, who reframe AI as a fall in the cost of *prediction*. Because prediction is an input to decision-making, the labour impact is ambiguous: automating prediction can substitute for workers (where prediction is the bottleneck) or complement them by raising the value of human *judgment* and surrounding tasks. The radiologist — AI predicting from images while the physician exercises judgment — is their canonical example.

Early experimental and observational evidence consistently shows large productivity gains on cognitive tasks with a skill-compressing pattern. The authors themselves stress limitations: narrow tasks, short horizons, no equilibrium adjustment, difficult quality measurement, and no direct mapping from task productivity to wages, employment, or the augment-versus-displace choice.

**Measurement challenges** pervade the field. Exposure indices measure *technical potential* or *task overlap*, not realised outcomes, and are explicitly not designed to predict equilibrium wages, employment, or whether AI augments or automates. **Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell & Restrepo**, studying the near-universe of US online vacancies from 2010, find that AI-exposed establishments adopting AI reduce non-AI hiring and change skill requirements, but that aggregate effects on employment and wages were, over their sample, too small to detect. This gap between exposure and outcomes is the central empirical uncertainty of the generative-AI transition.

### 4. Future Opportunities and Labour-Market Transformation

The strongest evidence on new-task creation is **Autor, Chin, Salomons & Seegmiller, "New Frontiers"**, who build an eight-decade database of new job titles linked to Census microdata and patent-based innovation measures. They find that "the majority of current employment is in new job specialties introduced after 1940," but that the locus of new-work creation has shifted — from middle-paid production and clerical occupations to high-paid professional and, secondarily, low-paid service occupations. Crucially, they distinguish *augmentation* innovations (which raise occupational labour demand) from *automation* innovations (which depress it), finding that "although flows of augmentation and automation innovations are positively correlated across occupations, the former boosts occupational labor demand while the latter depresses it," and that the demand-eroding effects of automation intensified over the last four decades while the demand-increasing effects of augmentation did not. New work is real and continual, but its recent composition and the intensifying force of automation are less favourable to labour than in the mid-twentieth century.

The **human–AI complementarity thesis** suggests the future turns on redesigning jobs so AI handles prediction/routine components while humans concentrate on judgment, interpersonal, and creative tasks. Brynjolfsson, Mitchell & Rock emphasise that capturing ML's value requires deliberate task-bundle redesign rather than wholesale occupational replacement. In medical imaging, **Ahn et al.**, in a cohort study of 497 chest radiographs read by six readers, found AI-aided interpretation reduced reporting time by about 10% (from 40.8 to 36.9 seconds per case) while improving reader sensitivity — a concrete demonstration of complementarity in a high-skilled occupation.

On **policy**, the most developed academic argument is Acemoglu & Restrepo's contention that the *direction* of AI development is a choice. Because markets under-reward employment creation (owing to innovation externalities and tax policies favouring capital over labour), there is no guarantee of an efficient balance between automating and augmenting AI; policy should redirect innovation toward the "right kind of AI" that creates new labour-intensive tasks. The skill-democratization findings suggest a complementary opportunity: if AI raises less-experienced workers' productivity most, it could compress inequality and lower entry barriers to cognitive occupations — provided institutions support reskilling and diffusion.

There is broad agreement that transitions will be disruptive even if aggregate employment is preserved: Bessen and Autor & Salomons stress that workers must move across industries, occupations, and geographies, and that these transitions can generate unemployment and wage losses for displaced groups. The policy consensus centres on education and training, portable social insurance, and redirecting AI toward augmentation.

### 5. Summary Table of Industry / Occupational Cases

| Study | Region | Occupation / Area | Application & Impact | Key Risks / Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Graetz & Michaels | 17 countries (EU/US/Asia) | Manufacturing (industrial robots) | Raised labour-productivity and TFP growth, lowered output prices; no significant fall in total hours | Reduced low-skilled employment share; observational; pre-AI robot era |
| Acemoglu & Restrepo | US local labour markets | Manufacturing / commuting-zone employment | One robot per 1,000 workers cut employment-to-population ratio 0.2 pp and wages 0.42% nationally (0.39 pp / 0.77% locally) | No offsetting local gains; robots ≠ AI (tangible vs intangible capital) |
| Autor & Salomons | US + Europe, 4 decades | Cross-industry (automation via TFP) | Automation displaces labour and cuts labour share in originating industry; some offset via demand and input–output linkages | Labour-share displacement intensified over time; TFP proxy is coarse |
| Bessen | US, ~200 years | Textile, steel, automotive | Automation raised industry employment when demand elastic; job losses only after demand satiation | Historical; modern demand elasticities differ across industries |
| Brynjolfsson, Li & Raymond | US (Fortune 500 firm) | Customer-support agents | Generative-AI assistant raised issues-resolved-per-hour 14% on average; 34% for novices; minimal for experts | Single firm; short horizon; tacit-knowledge diffusion mechanism suggestive |
| Noy & Zhang | US (online, n = 444) | Mid-level professional writing | ChatGPT cut time −0.8 SD, raised quality +0.4 SD, compressed productivity distribution | Narrow tasks; lab-style experiment; substitutes for effort more than skill |
| Peng et al. | US/global (95 freelance developers) | Software developers | GitHub Copilot users 55.8% faster (71.2 vs 160.9 min); larger gains for less-experienced/older | Single stylised coding task; no code-quality or long-run measure |
| Eloundou et al. | US workforce | White-collar / cognitive occupations | ~80% of workers could have ≥10% of tasks affected by LLMs; ~19% ≥50%; exposure rises with wage/education | Exposure ≠ realised outcomes; no adoption timeline; forward-looking |
| Acemoglu, Autor, Hazell & Restrepo | US (online vacancies) | AI-exposed establishments | AI adopters cut non-AI hiring and changed skill demands; aggregate wage/employment effects undetectable | Establishment-level only; early period; pre-generative-AI |
| Ahn et al. | US (Massachusetts General Hospital) | Radiology (chest X-ray) | AI-aided reading cut reporting time ~10% (40.8→36.9 s) and improved sensitivity | Small reader panel (n = 6); single-site; commercial tool |
| Webb | US | High-skilled occupations (patent-task overlap) | Projects AI directed at high-skilled tasks; could reduce 90:10 wage inequality | Working paper (later withdrawn); projections, not realised outcomes |
| Frey & Osborne | US (702 occupations) | Cross-occupational computerisation | ~47% of US employment estimated at high automation risk over 10–20 years | Occupation-level (not task-level); likely overstates automatability |

## Recommendations

**For researchers and analysts using this literature:**
- **Stage 1 — Distinguish exposure from outcome.** Treat exposure indices as measures of technical potential only. Pair every exposure claim with realised-outcome evidence. *Threshold that would change this:* the appearance of quasi-experimental studies detecting statistically significant aggregate wage/employment effects in LLM-exposed occupations would justify upgrading exposure findings to outcome claims.
- **Stage 2 — Weight by identification strength.** Give greatest evidentiary weight to RCTs and IV/quasi-experimental designs, and treat occupation-level susceptibility studies and projections as scenario-setting rather than forecasts.
- **Stage 3 — Separate the "robot" and "AI" evidence bases.** Do not transfer robot-displacement magnitudes to generative AI without justification, given the tangible/intangible capital distinction the authors themselves flag.

**For policymakers:**
- Prioritise **reskilling and transition support**, since even the optimistic studies predict disruptive cross-industry/occupation transitions rather than mass unemployment.
- Consider **redirecting the direction of AI innovation** toward augmentation and new-task creation, addressing the tax and externality distortions identified by Acemoglu & Restrepo that bias development toward "so-so" automation.
- **Leverage the skill-levelling opportunity:** because generative AI disproportionately raises the productivity of novices, targeted deployment could reduce inequality — a benchmark to monitor is whether within-occupation productivity dispersion narrows as adoption scales.

## Caveats

- **Strict temporal cutoff.** Several works were later revised or formally published after that date. These are cited in their working-paper/preprint versions, and the post-cutoff journal versions are deliberately **not** used. Webb was subsequently withdrawn and is flagged as a working paper.
- **Working papers and preprints carry more uncertainty** than peer-reviewed articles; the generative-AI evidence was largely un-refereed.
- **Forward-looking language.** Frey & Osborne, Webb, and Eloundou et al. present *projections and potentials*, not realised effects; their headline numbers describe what *could* happen under stated assumptions, not measured labour-market outcomes. This report presents them as such.
- **External validity limits.** The RCTs cover narrow tasks over short horizons; the robot studies concern manufacturing and predate modern AI; and single-firm/single-site studies may not generalise.
- **One near-miss excluded on date grounds.** The prominent experimental radiology paper by Agarwal, Moehring, Rajpurkar & Salz, "Combining Human Expertise with Artificial Intelligence," was released after the cutoff and is therefore **not** cited; the pre-cutoff Ahn et al. cohort study is used instead for the radiology case.
- **The optimistic and pessimistic findings are not necessarily contradictory:** they often describe different margins (average productivity vs distributional wage effects), different technologies (generative AI vs robots), and different time horizons (short-run experiments vs multi-decade structural change).

content.content:
# Literature Review: The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market

## Literature Review: The Restructuring Impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the Labor Market

### 1. Introduction

Artificial Intelligence is poised to revolutionize everything during the so-called Fourth Industrial Revolution, carrying several emerging technologies and progressing at unprecedented speed and scope. While governments, academia, industry, and civil society are all interested in understanding its multidimensional impact, the development and effects of AI are difficult to predict. Experts believe AI could bring tremendous benefits to humanity but also pose existential risks. During this emerging industrial revolution, the common good may be achieved through collaborative environments with shared interests, though implementing and monitoring projects globally will be challenging.

This literature review examines how Artificial Intelligence, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is restructuring the labor market. The review focuses on the disruptive effects of AI across various industries and their implications for employment, skills, and economic inequality.

### 2. AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution

#### 2.1 Definition and Context

Artificial intelligence represents one of the core areas of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, alongside the transformation of mechanical technology, electric power technology, and information technology. AI serves to promote the transformation and upgrading of the digital economy industry. The rapid iteration and cross-border integration of general information technology in the digital economy era has made significant contributions to employment stabilization and growth promotion.

The Fourth Industrial Revolution (FIR) represents an age of advanced technology based on information and communication. In the future, the super intelligence revolution based on the Internet of things, cyber-physical systems, and artificial intelligence will greatly change human intellectual labor. The technologies that will lead the FIR are diverse.

#### 2.2 Historical Context of Industrial Revolutions

The industrial revolution has historically led to changes in the labor market with machines replacing human labor. The first industrial revolution replaced manual work with the invention of the steam engine, and the second industrial revolution enabled mass production using electric energy. The tertiary industrial revolution started the automation era with informatization based on computers and the Internet.

### 3. AI's Impact on Employment and Job Displacement

#### 3.1 Projected Job Displacement

In the Fourth Industrial Revolution, STARA (smart technology, artificial intelligence, robotics, and algorithms) is predicted to replace a third of the jobs that exist today. It is forecast that by 2025, 85 million jobs may be displaced by a shift in the division of labor between humans and machines.

In general, STARA threatens to eliminate 47% of occupations.

Advances in artificial intelligence technologies are increasing machine capabilities to imitate human abilities. These advances have raised widespread concerns about labor market disruption, expressed in pioneering work by Frey and Osborne (2013). AI technologies have become pervasive across industries and are predicted to evolve into a general-purpose technology analogous to electricity's ubiquity and accessibility, while also having an exponentially greater impact on life and work. However, AI technologies are still in an early stage, far from the deployment of their full potential, and their effect on human work is very much in question. Discussions of AI impact focus substantially on how susceptible workers' tasks and jobs are to AI technologies. These advanced technologies can automate tasks and displace workers throughout the economy.

#### 3.2 Job Creation and New Roles

While 85 million jobs may be displaced by 2025, 97 million new roles may emerge that are more adapted to the new division of labor between humans, machines, and algorithms.

Artificial intelligence is already making its impact felt in the economy, public life, and day-to-day experiences. Although we're still in the very early chapters of the story, the general-purpose nature of the technology and its potential to be adopted quickly make AI hugely consequential, particularly in the world of work. The history of people working alongside machines, and those machines taking on some or all of the tasks originally carried out by workers, is a long one that will continue and accelerate. Machines will increasingly take on tasks that are currently the preserve of humans while also spurring innovation that creates new jobs that don't yet exist. This will lead employers to restructure entirely the ways in which workplaces operate, making the human impact apparent and increasingly obvious.

#### 3.3 Evidence from Recent Studies

Recent empirical investigations into the impact of Artificial Intelligence on employment have found concerning trends. During 2010-2021, commuting zones with higher AI adoption experienced a stronger decline in the employment-to-population ratio. This negative employment effect is primarily borne by the manufacturing and low-skill services sectors, middle-skill workers, non-STEM occupations, and individuals at the two ends of the age distribution.

Driven by the rapid development of artificial intelligence technology, some enterprises have accelerated the pace of "machine replacement," resulting in repetitive and standardized jobs being performed by robots. Deep learning and AI enable machines and operating systems to perform more complex tasks, and the employment prospects of enterprise employees face new challenges in the digital economy. According to the Future of Jobs 2020 report released by the World Economic Forum, the recession caused by the COVID-19 pandemic and the rapid development of automation technology are changing the job market much faster than expected, and automation and the new division of labor between humans and machines will disrupt 85 million jobs in 15 industries worldwide over the next five years. The demand for skilled jobs, such as data entry, accounting, and administrative services, has been hard hit.

### 4. Impact on Various Industries and Occupations

#### 4.1 Healthcare and Medical Fields

AI could have a substantial impact on healthcare through applications such as robots for surgery.

Professions currently enjoying relatively high wages and high employment stability are likely to be replaced by AI and robots. Artificial intelligence Watson and remote telesurgery robots like Da-Vinci will replace human medical practices.

#### 4.2 Legal Profession

Legal information can be easily accessed by the general public. In addition, standardized legal systems and the development of technology can threaten the work of lawyers.

In a first look, jobs such as coders, software developers, computer programmers, and data scientists are at risk of being displaced by AI. In addition, legal industry jobs such as paralegals and legal assistants are susceptible to AI-driven change, as they consume large amounts of information, synthesize what they learned, and make it digestible through a legal brief or opinion.

#### 4.3 Education and Knowledge Work

......
AI can augment workers' capabilities and increase access to work for differently abled workers and other marginalized groups. This could reduce inequality by ensuring a more meritocratic division of opportunities. AI-user surveys indicate that workers tend to look at AI as an opportunity to upskill and improve job security and advancement. A fulfillment-center supervisor who worked in the retail industry noted that automated robots displaced some roles but overall led him to learn new technological skills and enjoy his job much more because, "I feel more valued; before… I felt that I was the robot." AI tools also enable differently abled workers to gain access to workplaces that may otherwise have been inaccessible.

Materialized AI technology can improve the total factor production efficiency in ways suitable for its factor endowment structure and improve the production efficiency between upstream and downstream enterprises in the industrial chain and the value chain. This increase in the efficiency of the entire market will subsequently drive the expansion of the production scale of enterprises and promote reproduction, and its synergy will promote the synchronous growth of labor demand involving various skills, thus resulting in a creative effect. As an essential force in the fourth industrial revolution, AI inevitably affects the social status of humans and changes the structure of the labor force. AI and machines increase labor productivity by automating routine tasks while expanding employee skills and increasing the value of work.

### 7. Policy Implications and Responses

#### 7.1 Education and Training

According to Autor (2022), technological change creates winners and losers, but complementary institutional investments are needed to generate shared gains. The author highlights three domains for policy intervention: education and training, labor market institutions, and innovation policy. Improving the workforce's skills, revitalizing labor market institutions, and directly shaping innovation to complement the workforce's skills are all potential avenues for policy intervention. The author concludes by suggesting that policy reforms are necessary to align the benefits of technological innovation with shared prosperity.

Since AI learning tools remain relatively new, there is a dearth of in-depth academic studies and long-running, large-scale pilot programs to quantify their potential impact on learners and the wider economy. Analysis suggests AI could boost educational attainment levels by around 6 percent on average over students' academic careers through a combination of improved attainment of individual students and by enabling more students to progress to higher levels of education. Importantly, the benefits from AI tend to be bigger for lower-performing students, suggesting that AI-enabled education could help improve equality of opportunity. By boosting the productivity of the future workforce, AI-enabled education could raise GDP by around 6 percent in the long run and add more than 0.1 percent to growth per year for more than 40 years.

#### 7.2 Labor Market Policies and Social Safety Nets

There is much to be alarmed about when it comes to AI. But if AI brings a dystopian future, it will not be because this was the only path available to us. It will be because we failed to grasp how this technology could be developed and used, and how it could help workers, rather than just replacing them. Understanding this is a first step toward the right type of AI revolution.

Lawmakers should revise the federal tax code to equalize the tax burden across labor and machines, so companies are encouraged to hire, train, and retain human workers. Regulatory agencies should also find ways to include and amplify worker voice on how AI and other technology is used in the workplace—including regulating AI-assisted personnel management and placing safeguards around workplace surveillance. The public sector should invest in research that prioritizes human-complementary AI technology and create a federal government consultative AI center of expertise to support lawmakers and officials who need to understand this technology.

#### 7.3 Ethical Considerations and Governance

Research has identified several ethical issues raised by the introduction of artificial intelligence at work. These issues include: (1) How to govern the impact of AI on job losses and other social issues raised by the reshaping of the job market? (2) AI may contribute to creating new forms of oppression and violation of rights of the workforce; (3) AI may negatively affect workers' (moral) agency, autonomy or responsibility; (4) AI may create hidden labor, that is, economically valuable tasks are performed by human agents without their work being sufficiently recognized, rewarded or protected, with technology companies acquiring an unfair gain and increasing socio-economic power over people.

In most scenarios, AI will likely worsen overall inequality, a troubling trend that policymakers must proactively address to prevent the technology from further stoking social tensions. It is crucial for countries to establish comprehensive social safety nets and offer retraining programs for vulnerable workers. In doing so, we can make the AI transition more inclusive, protecting livelihoods and curbing inequality.

### 8. Conclusion

We strongly expect continuing technical progress in AI and expect it to significantly impact the economy and the workforce. The future we'll live in is not destined by technology—it will be determined by the decisions we make about how to use it. AI is likely to automate routine tasks and partially automate high-skill tasks. With the right tools and guidance, AI could help workers whose jobs require judgment, problem-solving, and decision making do their jobs better. Policies should support research on human-complementary AI, how business decisions about AI are made, and how to ensure workers retain control and compensation for creative work.

The literature on AI's impact on the labor market reveals a complex and multifaceted transformation underway. While AI promises productivity gains and economic growth, it also poses significant challenges in terms of job displacement, skill requirements, and inequality. The research suggests that AI will not simply replace human workers but will reshape the nature of work itself, requiring new skills and creating new opportunities.

Yet the future is not preordained. Ultimately, whether workers benefit from AI-driven productivity gains or suffer harm and precarity depends in part on the ability of workers and other stakeholders to shape the technology's deployment, as well as the specific choices that employers, technology companies, policymakers, consumers, and civil society make.

Policymakers, businesses, and educational institutions must collaborate to ensure that the benefits of AI are widely shared, that workers are adequately prepared for the changing nature of work, and that appropriate social protections are in place for those negatively affected by these transformations. The Fourth Industrial Revolution driven by AI presents both unprecedented challenges and opportunities for reshaping labor markets in a more inclusive and sustainable direction.

Regulatory agencies should also find ways to include and amplify worker voice on how AI and other technology is used in the workplace—including regulating AI-assisted personnel management and placing safeguards around workplace surveillance. The public sector should invest in research that prioritizes human-complementary AI technology and create a federal government consultative AI center of expertise to support lawmakers and officials who need to understand this technology.

#### 7.3 Ethical Considerations and Governance

Research has identified several ethical issues raised by the introduction of artificial intelligence at work. These issues include: (1) How to govern the impact of AI on job losses and other social issues raised by the reshaping of the job market? (2) AI may contribute to creating new forms of oppression and violation of rights of the workforce; (3) AI may negatively affect workers' (moral) agency, autonomy or responsibility; (4) AI may create hidden labor, that is, economically valuable tasks are performed by human agents without their work being sufficiently recognized, rewarded or protected, with technology companies acquiring an unfair gain and increasing socio-economic power over people.

In most scenarios, AI will likely worsen overall inequality, a troubling trend that policymakers must proactively address to prevent the technology from further stoking social tensions. It is crucial for countries to establish comprehensive social safety nets and offer retraining programs for vulnerable workers. In doing so, we can make the AI transition more inclusive, protecting livelihoods and curbing inequality.

### 8. Conclusion

We strongly expect continuing technical progress in AI and expect it to significantly impact the economy and the workforce. The future we'll live in is not destined by technology—it will be determined by the decisions we make about how to use it. AI is likely to automate routine tasks and partially automate high-skill tasks. With the right tools and guidance, AI could help workers whose jobs require judgment, problem-solving, and decision making do their jobs better. Policies should support research on human-complementary AI, how business decisions about AI are made, and how to ensure workers retain control and compensation for creative work.

The literature on AI's impact on the labor market reveals a complex and multifaceted transformation underway. While AI promises productivity gains and economic growth, it also poses significant challenges in terms of job displacement, skill requirements, and inequality. The research suggests that AI will not simply replace human workers but will reshape the nature of work itself, requiring new skills and creating new opportunities.

Yet the future is not preordained. Ultimately, whether workers benefit from AI-driven productivity gains or suffer harm and precarity depends in part on the ability of workers and other stakeholders to shape the technology's deployment, as well as the specific choices that employers, technology companies, policymakers, consumers, and civil society make.

Policymakers, businesses, and educational institutions must collaborate to ensure that the benefits of AI are widely shared, that workers are adequately prepared for the changing nature of work, and that appropriate social protections are in place for those negatively affected by these transformations. The Fourth Industrial Revolution driven by AI presents both unprecedented challenges and opportunities for reshaping labor markets in a more inclusive and sustainable direction.
